era_context: The Roman Empire under Hadrian (r. 117–138 CE) is at its territorial zenith and in the midst of cultural and architectural patronage. The Eastern Han dynasty in China is experiencing increasing court instability and eunuch influence. The Kushan Empire dominates Central Asia and the Silk Road trade routes. The year before the Bar Kokhba Revolt.

events.title: Hadrian's travels: continued tours of the empire
events.description: Hadrian continues his extensive travels throughout the Roman Empire in 131 CE. Known for his peripatetic style of rule, Hadrian visits various provinces, inspects military installations, and participates in cultural and religious ceremonies. His travels serve both administrative and cultural purposes, allowing him to maintain direct contact with the empire's diverse regions and populations. By 131 CE, Hadrian has already visited most major provinces and is consolidating his cultural legacy through architectural patronage and religious reforms.

sources.name: Scriptores Historiae Augustae (Historia Augusta), Life of Hadrian
Archaeological evidence from various sites throughout the empire

events.certainty_note: Hadrian's extensive travels are well-attested, but the specific itinerary for 131 CE is not always clear from sources. The year is assigned by convention as part of his broader travel program.
cross_references: Part of Hadrian's broader travels throughout the empire (r. 117–138 CE)
Reflects the cultural and administrative dimensions of Roman imperial power

events.title: Eastern Han court: continued eunuch ascendancy and factional struggles
events.description: The Eastern Han court continues to be dominated by factional struggles between eunuchs, imperial consorts' families, and scholar-officials. By 131 CE, the pattern of child emperors and regents is well-established, with eunuchs wielding increasing power. The imperial succession remains unstable, and court intrigues are frequent. This period marks the continuation of the institutional decline that will eventually lead to the dynasty's collapse in 220 CE.

sources.name: Hou Hanshu (History of the Later Han), compiled 5th century CE

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of court instability and eunuch ascendancy in the 2nd century CE is well-established. The specific year 131 CE is assigned by convention; the exact chronology of individual court events is less certain.
cross_references: Part of the decline of the Eastern Han dynasty
Contemporary with Roman political stability under Hadrian (contrast)

events.title: Kushan Empire: height of power and cultural influence
events.description: The Kushan Empire, under Kanishka I (r. c. 127–150 CE) or his successors, is at the height of its power and cultural influence. The empire dominates the Central Asian trade routes and serves as a crucial conduit for the transmission of Buddhism along the Silk Road. Kushan coins, bearing inscriptions in Greek, Persian, and Indian languages, reflect the empire's multicultural character. Buddhist monasteries and stupas are patronized throughout the empire, and the Kushans facilitate cultural and commercial exchange between the Roman Empire, China, and India.
key_figures: Kanishka I (Kushan emperor, r. c. 127–150 CE)
Buddhist monks and scholars patronized by the Kushan court

sources.name: Chinese historical records (Hou Hanshu, compiled later)

events.certainty_note: The Kushan Empire's dominance of Central Asia in the 2nd century CE is well-established. However, the exact dating of Kanishka's reign is debated. The year 131 CE is assigned by convention as a marker within this period of Kushan prominence.
cross_references: Contemporary with Roman-Parthian tensions over trade routes
Facilitates the spread of Buddhism to China and beyond
Part of the broader Silk Road trade network

events.title: Judaea under Roman rule: tensions building before the Bar Kokhba Revolt
events.description: In 131 CE, Judaea is under Roman rule following the suppression of the First Jewish-Roman War (66–70 CE). Tensions are building in the province due to Hadrian's plans to rebuild Jerusalem as a pagan city (Aelia Capitolina) and to erect a temple to Jupiter Capitolinus on the site of the Jewish Temple. These plans are deeply offensive to the Jewish population and will trigger the Bar Kokhba Revolt in 132 CE. By 131 CE, the Jewish population is increasingly restive, and resistance to Roman rule is growing.
events.certainty_note: The tensions in Judaea in 131 CE are inferred from the events of 132 CE and later sources. The specific conditions in 131 CE are not extensively documented, but the broader context is clear.
cross_references: Leads to the Bar Kokhba Revolt in 132 CE
Follows from the First Jewish-Roman War (66–70 CE)

disconfirming_evidence: The exact dating of Hadrian's travels in 131 CE is not always clear from sources; the Historia Augusta and other sources provide general information about his travels but do not always specify the exact year. The dating of Kanishka's reign is one of the most contentious issues in Indian history, with scholars proposing dates ranging from c. 78 CE to c. 150 CE. The tensions in Judaea in 131 CE are inferred from later events rather than directly documented.
historiographic_note: The year 131 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire, with multiple sources available for major events. However, the record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events. For China, the historical record is rich but filtered through later compilations; contemporary records from 131 CE itself are sparse. For Judaea, the historical record is limited for this specific year, with most information coming from sources describing the Bar Kokhba Revolt that begins in 132 CE. The experiences of ordinary people, women, and non-elite populations are largely absent. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas have no synchronized written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: The tensions in Judaea in 131 CE lead to the Bar Kokhba Revolt in 132 CE
Hadrian's travels are part of his broader administrative and cultural program (r. 117–138 CE)
The institutional decline of the Eastern Han contributes to the dynasty's collapse in 220 CE
